model_summary: This initial observation model targets recurring nightmare awakenings and their proximate mechanisms: nightmare intensity, anticipatory sleep fear, acute autonomic arousal at awakening, prolonged post-awakening activation, and difficulty returning to sleep. It pairs six criterion variables (captured primarily with a morning sleep-diary and a single nightly pre-sleep EMA) with seven actionable predictors spanning trauma-focused imagery rehearsal, presleep wind-down/routine adherence, post-awakening grounding/paced breathing, sleep-environment optimization, HRV/breathing biofeedback practice + passive HRV, stimulus-control (non-clock checking/bedroom associations), and wearable-based monitoring/feedback. Measures prioritize 1–9 anchored Likert scales to maximize within-person variance, use morning-diary anchors for last-night phenomena, and combine passive wearable signals for objective arousal/awakening markers. The design is conservatively sized for person-level gVAR (daily resolution + 3 momentary prompts) over 28 days to produce sufficient time series (>=120 observations) while minimizing collinearity and redundancy.
variable_selection_notes: The criteria set preserves the complaint's resolution (nightmares, realistic vividness, anticipatory fear, sweating/heart racing, long calming period, trouble returning to sleep). Chosen predictors emphasize high HyDe/LLM signals (imagery rehearsal, presleep routines, breathing/grounding, environment, HRV biofeedback, stimulus control, and monitoring) that are modifiable and measurable at daily/nightly resolution. Trade-offs: limited number of predictors (7) to keep gVAR estimable while covering major mechanisms (cognitive imagery content, pre-sleep conditioning, autonomic downregulation, environmental contributors, and monitoring/adherence). Redundancy reduced by selecting one broad self-regulation action (presleep wind-down) rather than many overlapping constructs. Uncertainty/bias notes: estimated relevance uses complaint text + ranking signals and domain plausibility; some effects (e.g., environment -> nightmare vividness) are weaker and possibly indirect. Passive HR/HRV may be noisy (device differences) — transformation and quality-control steps are recommended.

time_blocks_local: 08:00-09:30 (morning brief check if not completed diary)
momentary_ema.recall_window: since last prompt / today (for daytime) ; pre-sleep anchors for bedtime prompt
momentary_ema.notes: Three daily prompts primarily for capturing bedtime anticipatory fear (22:00-23:30), daytime context/adherence, and a fallback morning brief if diary missed. Morning diary is primary for last-night events (see daily_diary).

anchor_times_local: upon_waking (morning diary within 60 minutes of final awakening)
daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures last-night nightmare occurrence, vividness, autonomic signs, time-to-calm, and difficulty resuming sleep. If participant wakes from a nightmare and is willing, an event-contingent brief can be triggered (optional).

passive_streams.notes: Continuous heart rate (and if available HRV estimates), accelerometry/motion, and device-detected sleep/awakening events. Aggregate features produced per night and aligned to morning diary; per-awakening windows (±5 min) flagged for autonomic spikes.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: With 28 daily diaries and three scheduled EMAs (one of which is bedtime) the design yields ~112+ observations; combined with passive nightly aggregates this meets minimal person-level gVAR sample size recommendations (>100 timepoints). Maintain consistent timestamps and reduce missingness to preserve temporal ordering.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 anchored Likert scales for subjective items to increase within-person variance. For time-to-calm use an ordinal 1–9 anchored to minute ranges (e.g., 1=0-2min ... 9=>60min) to avoid heavy right-skew. Passive HR/HRV use log or winsorization for spikes.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: missed morning diaries after poor sleep and participant burden. Mitigation: short morning diary (5 items), optional event-triggered single-item after nightmare, passive detection to fill gaps, reminder nudges, and accept planned missingness for occasional days.

safety_notes: This model is not a clinical diagnosis. If nightmares escalate, cause functional deterioration, or if there is any emergence of suicidal ideation or safety concerns, advise immediate contact with a mental health professional or emergency services. Imagery and trauma-focused work can be distressing — recommend starting slowly and having a clinician contact option if symptoms worsen. Ensure participant consent and secure handling of biometric data.

criteria_variables.label: Nightmare-triggered awakenings (occurrence)

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, did you wake because of a nightmare? (0=No ; 1=Yes). If yes: How many nightmare awakenings did you have last night? (enter number) AND How much did the nightmare interrupt your sleep overall? (1–9; 1=not at all, 9=completely)
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: binary flag + integer count + 1–9 interruption severity
measurement.notes: Primary event flag and intensity score combined to avoid binary-only measurement; use count when >0.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: use counts as-is (rare) with a companion severity 1–9 (z-score across series); consider event-flag logistic gVAR or include severity continuous

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / medication or substance-induced sleep-wake disorders / medication or substance-induced parasomnias / medication-associated nightmare disorder / *nocturnal_awakenings_due_to_nightmares
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: variable label: Nightmare-triggered awakenings (occurrence); measurement: EMA_self_report via morning diary with last-night events (Last night, did you wake because of a nightmare? + count + interference). The leaf nocturnal_awakenings_due_to_nightmares directly encodes awakenings caused by nightmares and is the closest, most specific operational match among candidates for this composite nocturnal nightmare event measure. Other options (REM-specific nightmares, trauma-related nightmares) are not as general or applicable to the variable’s broad scope.

criteria_variables.label: Nightmare vividness/intensity

measurement.item_or_signal: If you had a nightmare last night, how vivid/real did it feel? Rate 1–9 (1=not vivid/like a film, 9=extremely vivid and real)
measurement.notes: If no nightmare flagged, set as missing or 1 with explicit 'no nightmare' flag; prefer severity-only for gVAR continuity.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score within-person; handle missingness when no nightmare occurred

This provides the most specific operational construct for the intended variable within the available leaves.

criteria_variables.label: Anticipatory fear of sleep / reluctance to go to bed

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now (pre-sleep): How fearful or worried are you about going to sleep because of possible nightmares? Rate 1–9 (1=not at all, 9=extremely fearful)
measurement.notes: Anchored at bedtime to capture anticipatory fear; also capture planned bedtime and sleep intention as optional contextual items.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score within-person

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / chronic insomnia disorder / sleep-onset insomnia / *bedtime_anxiety_present
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable C03 encodes anticipatory fear/worry about going to sleep due to possible nightmares, assessed momentarily at bedtime via EMA. The closest leaf is bedtime_anxiety_present (sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / sleep-onset insomnia / bedtime_anxiety_present), which represents pre-sleep anxiety at the time of trying to sleep and is aligned with the measure's timing, scale (1–9), and intent. This leaf is more specific than broader nightmare-related or general anxiety nodes and matches the pre-sleep cognitive arousal mechanism targeted by the model.

criteria_variables.label: Acute autonomic arousal on awakening

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary) + passive wearable HR
measurement.item_or_signal: When you woke from the nightmare last night, did you experience sweating or racing heart? Rate intensity 1–9 (1=none, 9=extreme). Additionally: wearable-derived peak heart rate in 5-minute window after awakening (bpm) and HRV short-term estimate (RMSSD when available).
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1–9 Likert + beats per minute + HRV estimate (ms)
measurement.notes: Combine subjective intensity with objective passive spikes aligned to self-reported awakening (±5 min).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: objective HR use log1p for spikes; subjective intensity z-score

This aligns with both the subjective EMA item (sweating/heart racing during awakening) and objective wearable signals (peak HR and RMSSD after awakening). Among candidates, this leaf provides the most specific operational construct for autonomic arousal during awakening from a nightmare, while fitting the measurement modalities described (morning diary EMA + passive HR/HRV data).

criteria_variables.label: Prolonged post-awakening physiological/psychological activation

measurement.item_or_signal: How long did it take you to feel calm/settled after last night's nightmare awakening? Rate 1–9 where 1=0–2 minutes, 5=15–30 minutes, 9=>60 minutes
measurement.notes: Time-to-calm approximated to ordinal bins to reduce skew; can also capture whether participant ultimately returned to sleep last night (yes/no).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: treat as ordinal continuous then z-score; consider log-transform if converting to minutes

criteria_variables.label: Difficulty resuming sleep after nightmare awakenings

measurement.item_or_signal: After waking from the nightmare, how difficult was it to fall back asleep? Rate 1–9 (1=fell back asleep immediately, 9=couldn't fall back asleep for long time or not at all). Also: did you eventually return to sleep? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Use continuous 1–9 as primary gVAR input; binary flag used for event modeling if needed.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

predictor_variables.label: Imagery Rehearsal (nightmare imagery rescripting) practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Imagery rehearsal modifies nightmare content and reduces frequency/vividness by providing an alternative memory script and inhibitory learning prior to sleep.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Perform 10–15 min imagery rehearsal earlier in the evening: rehearse an edited, less threatening version of the nightmare imagery.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, did you do imagery rehearsal (practice changing nightmare content)? (Yes/No). If yes: How fully did you practice it? Rate 1–9 (1=brief/partial, 9=complete session). How many minutes did you practice?
measurement.notes: Primary behavioral predictor; measure both adherence and intensity.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score adherence intensity; minutes log1p if used

expected_effects.rationale: Imagery rehearsal performed pre-sleep typically reduces next-night nightmare frequency via cognitive updating (effects may appear within 1-2 nights).
Directly targets nightmare content vividness; effect appears on subsequent nights' vividness ratings.
Rehearsal may reduce anticipatory fear at bedtime by increasing perceived control over dreams.
Indirectly may reduce autonomic intensity by altering nightmare content; effect size expected smaller and less immediate.
By reducing nightmare severity, time-to-calm after awakenings may shorten.
Lowered intensity/fear should make resuming sleep easier.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Imagery work can be distressing for some; start with brief practice and provide support resources if distress increases.

feasibility.notes: Self-report adherence is straightforward; effects are episodic but measurable nightly.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Direct mapping to the target variable: nightmare_imagery_rehearsal leaf under imagery_and_rescripting_interventions. The variable label explicitly names Imagery Rehearsal (nightmare imagery rescripting) practice, with EMA_self_report on presleep/evening and items about whether practice occurred, adherence intensity, and minutes. This leaf uniquely captures the intended presleep nightmare imagery rehearsal intervention and aligns with the mechanism (modifying nightmare content through rehearsal). It is more specific than broader imagery_rescripting_framework or other imagery-related leaves.

predictor_variables.label: Presleep wind-down / sleep routine adherence
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Consistent presleep routines reduce physiological/cognitive arousal and conditioned anticipatory anxiety, improving sleep maintenance.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 30–60 min wind-down (low-stimulation activities, no screens, low lighting, gentle reading or audiobook).

measurement.item_or_signal: Tonight, how well did you follow your wind-down routine? Rate 1–9 (1=not at all, 9=fully). Did you avoid stimulating content and devices in the hour before bed? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Routine adherence is a proximal modifiable predictor; use both adherence intensity and binary avoidance items.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; binary items as proportion over windows

expected_effects.rationale: Better wind-down reduces general arousal and may reduce night awakenings.
May modestly reduce dream vividness by lowering presleep cognitive activation.
Directly reduces anticipatory fear via calming routines.
Lower baseline arousal reduces peak autonomic response probability/intensity.
Calmer baseline may shorten time-to-calm after awakenings.
Improved sleep drive and conditioning makes returning to sleep easier.

feasibility.notes: Simple nightly self-report; high adherence expected if kept brief.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf is presleep_downshift / wind_down_routine because the variable label is Presleep wind-down / sleep routine adherence, and the measurement item directly asks how well the wind-down routine was followed (1–9). This leaf represents the most specific operational construct for routine adherence in the presleep_downshift domain. The EMA self-report measurement and the routine-adherence focus align with the measurement mode/item. Mechanistically, presleep wind-down is intended to reduce arousal and anticipatory anxiety, matching the proposed mechanism. The pseudoprofile context treats this as a proximal, modifiable predictor. Other leaves target stimulation avoidance or alternative presleep activities, but wind_down_routine is the closest match to the variable as a whole.

predictor_variables.label: Post-awakening grounding and paced-breathing practice
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Grounding and paced breathing applied immediately after awakening target autonomic downregulation, reducing heart-rate spikes and shortening time-to-calm.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 3–10 minutes of 6-breath-per-minute paced breathing and grounding (5-4-3-2-1 sensory check), performed on awakening.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary) + optional event-trigger
measurement.item_or_signal: After the awakening, did you use grounding or paced breathing? (Yes/No). If yes: Rate how long/intense (1–9; 1=brief, 9=full practice).
measurement.notes: Event-tied practice expected to affect immediate arousal metrics; capture timing relative to awakening when possible.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score intensity; use binary flag for event analysis

expected_effects.rationale: Unlikely to reduce occurrence of nightmares (which originate during REM) but may influence downstream outcomes; therefore effect on C01 is uncertain.
Direct influence on nightmare vividness less likely; immediate effect is arousal reduction.
Practicing grounding may reduce anticipatory fear by increasing perceived coping ability in later nights.
Direct autonomic downregulation reduces heart rate/sweating intensity within minutes.
Shortens time-to-calm by lowering physiological arousal quickly.
Facilitates returning to sleep by reducing activation and intrusive imagery.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: If practice increases distress for some, reduce duration and provide alternative grounding prompts.

feasibility.notes: Event-linked measure; may miss some awakenings if participant delayed diary entry—encourage quick post-awakening reporting.

Among candidates, the closest exact match is paced_breathing_practice (a specific breath-and-relaxation skill) under breath_and_relaxation_skills within PSYCHO, aligning with the measurement item (Yes/No + intensity) and the morning diary context. This mapping uses the measurement mode (EMA_self_report) and the last-awakening recall window appropriately.

predictor_variables.label: Sleep environment optimization (light/noise/temperature/comfort)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Reducing environmental disruptions and improving comfort reduces likelihood of awakenings and facilitates returning to sleep; may also reduce conditioned arousal.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Use blackout curtains, lower bedroom temperature, reduce ambient noise, remove screens from bedroom, use low-path night lights for bathroom.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary) + optional passive phone ambient light/noise if available
measurement.item_or_signal: Rate last night's bedroom comfort/optimization (temperature, light, noise, mattress) overall 1–9 (1=very poor, 9=excellent). Optional: was a low-light bathroom path used? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Passive ambient noise/light measures can augment self-report when available.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; treat as slowly-varying covariate if largely constant

expected_effects.rationale: Fewer environmental disruptors make awakenings less likely and reduce secondary awakenings.
Effect on dream vividness is indirect and likely small (hence nonlinear/uncertain).
A comfortable environment reduces pre-sleep vigilance and anticipatory fear.
Lower external triggers can reduce reflexive autonomic responses on awakening.
Better sleep environment supports faster return to baseline after awakening.
Comfort and reduced light/noise support falling back asleep sooner.

feasibility.notes: If environment is near-constant, may show low variance—encourage small nightly changes to increase signal.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is BIO / environmental_exposures_and_built_environment_optimization / thermal_comfort_and_shelter_integrity / indoor_sleep_comfort_environment / bedding_thermal_optimization. The var label Sleep environment optimization and the EMA self-report measure (rating last night's bedroom comfort/optimization including temperature, light, noise, mattress) map directly to environmental comfort and bedding thermals. The mechanism is that reducing environmental disruptions lowers awakenings and facilitates returning to sleep. This is the most comprehensive environmental predictor among candidates (others target specific aspects like blue light, blackout, or mattress alone) and fits the morning diary assessment window. Pseudoprofile context provides general neurological/physiological arousal and sleep initiation dynamics but is not overfitting the measurement choice.

predictor_variables.label: HRV / slow-breathing biofeedback practice and passive HRV monitoring
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Regular HRV/slow-breathing practice increases parasympathetic tone and reduces autonomic reactivity; passive HR/HRV tracks physiological arousal during/after awakenings.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Daily 10-minute paced-breathing biofeedback or guided HRV exercises; wearable records nightly HR and short-term HRV (RMSSD).

measurement.channel: wearable device (heart rate, accelerometry) + smartphone app for practice logging
measurement.item_or_signal: Passive: nightly mean HR, peak HR in awakening windows, and RMSSD per-night. EMA: Did you practice HRV biofeedback today? (Yes/No) If yes, duration minutes and perceived effectiveness 1–9.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: HR bpm, HRV ms; binary + minutes + 1–9
measurement.recall_window: daily aggregate (last 24 hours) and per-awakening windows
measurement.notes: Objective physiological measures align with subjective intensity items for C04 and C05.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: RMSSD use log or winsorize; BPM subject to z-score and remove measurement artifacts

expected_effects.rationale: Training may reduce baseline reactivity over days, possibly lowering awakening probability indirectly; effect timeline can be multi-day.
Little direct effect on dream content, likely small/indirect.
Improved physiological control can reduce anticipatory hyperarousal at bedtime.
Immediate practice before bed or on awakening can lower autonomic spikes; passive HRV correlates with arousal intensity.
Improved parasympathetic tone shortens time-to-calm.
Lower arousal supports faster resumption of sleep.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure wearable data privacy; advise pause if biofeedback practice increases anxiety.

feasibility.notes: Passive data dependent on participant having compatible wearable; otherwise use self-report practice logs.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf: HRV biofeedback device. The variable combines HRV metrics collected by wearables (nightly HR, RMSSD) with a paced-breathing biofeedback component (biofeedback practice). The leaf 'hrv_biofeedback_device' explicitly captures a wearable biofeedback device for HRV-based autonomic regulation, aligning with both passive HRV monitoring and active biofeedback training described in the variable. Other candidates (hrv_tracker, hrv_guided_breathing_tool, chest-strap_hrv, etc.) are more narrow (tracking only, or guided breathing only) and do not fully encompass both the passive HRV monitoring and active HRV biofeedback component described.

EMA: Did you practice HRV biofeedback today? (Yes/No) If yes, duration minutes and perceived effectiveness 1–9. | Prior ontology hint: BIO / neuromodulation_and_neurostimulation / biofeedback_and_neurofeedback

predictor_variables.label: Stimulus-control / non-clock-checking and bed association
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Reducing bed-related worry/clock checking and re-establishing bed as sleep-only environment reduces conditioned arousal and anticipatory anxiety.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Avoid checking clock/phone when awake at night; get out of bed if unable to return to sleep and perform a quiet activity.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary / event if awakened)
measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, did you check the clock or your phone during awakenings? (Yes/No). How often? Rate 1–9 frequency (1=never, 9=very often). Did you get out of bed if unable to sleep? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Stimulus control behaviors directly map to conditioned arousal and difficulty returning to sleep.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: binary behaviors as proportions; frequency as z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Reducing active engagement/clock-checking may reduce secondary awakenings and conditioned awakening responses.
Effect on vividness is indirect and small.
Reduces bedtime hypervigilance and threat-checking behaviors.
Reducing checking can attenuate sympathetic activation triggered by threat cues (clock/time anxiety).
Less checking and better stimulus control supports faster return to sleep.
Direct behavioral pathway to improve resumption of sleep.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Encourage safe behaviors (avoid wandering alone if distressed).

feasibility.notes: Self-report items easy to collect nightly; passive phone unlock logs could augment if consent provided.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-match leaf: stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning. The variable is labeled as Stimulus-control / non-clock-checking and bed association, which directly corresponds to a CBT-I oriented stimulus-control intervention. The measurement items (Last night, did you check the clock or your phone during awakenings? How often? 1–9; Did you get out of bed if unable to sleep?) align with EMA/self-report morning diary capture of clock-checking and bed-exit behaviors. Mechanism: re-establishing bed as sleep-only to reduce conditioned arousal and anticipatory worry. Pseudoprofile context notes this predictor among seven action-oriented factors; the measurement mode (EMA via smartphone, morning diary) and unit (binary plus 1–9 frequency) match the operational construct closely.

predictor_variables.label: Wearable sleep/arousal monitoring with feedback
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Objective detection of awakenings and autonomic spikes supports measurement-based feedback and may improve adherence to coping strategies when used for self-monitoring.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Wear a wrist sensor to capture sleep/wake, accelerometry, and heart rate; receive nightly summary with detected awakenings and peak HR metrics.

measurement.item_or_signal: Per-night: number of device-detected awakenings, total awake minutes, peak HR in awakening windows (bpm), nightly mean HR, and estimated sleep stages if available.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: counts (awakenings), minutes awake, bpm, HRV when available
measurement.recall_window: nightly aggregate aligned to morning diary
measurement.notes: Monitoring itself may change behavior (reactivity); include self-report of whether feedback was reviewed each day.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: handle device-specific offsets; z-score nightly metrics, winsorize extreme spikes

expected_effects.rationale: Feedback may indirectly reduce awakenings by informing behavior changes; direct measurement maps well to this criterion.
Wearable data does not directly measure dream vividness; possible indirect effects via behavior change.
Seeing objective data may reduce catastrophizing and anticipatory fear via psychoeducation/feedback.
Objective detection of HR spikes maps to autonomic arousal and can inform immediate downregulation strategies.
Feedback can prompt use of quicker regulation strategies, reducing time-to-calm.
Monitoring+feedback supports behavior change (stimulus control, routines) and may improve resumption of sleep.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure privacy and informed consent for continuous biometric data; data may cause rumination in some users—monitor for increased worry.

feasibility.notes: Device availability and data quality vary; include fallbacks to self-report if passive data missing.

The variable Wearable sleep/arousal monitoring with feedback maps to a wearable sleep-tracking device that detects awakenings and HR-related arousal at nightly resolution and can deliver feedback to the user, aligning with per-night measurements ( awakenings, minutes awake, peak HR, nightly mean HR, sleep stages ) and the measurement mode (wearable_sensor) with feedback context in the pseudoprofile.

expected_collinearity_risks: P01 Imagery Rehearsal vs P02 Presleep routine: moderate correlation if imagery incorporated into wind-down.
P02 Presleep routine vs P06 Stimulus control: moderate correlation (both capture presleep behaviors).
P05 HRV practice vs P03 grounding/paced breathing: moderate correlation due to overlapping breathing techniques.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: P04 Sleep environment may show low within-person variance if participant does not change environment during study (ceiling).
P07 wearable metrics may have floor effects for HRV if device fails to capture reliable signal.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Encourage small nightly variation (e.g., alternate wind-down activities) to increase within-person variance for P02 and P04.
Use both binary adherence and graded intensity (1–9) to capture partial engagement and increase continuous variance.
Include event-contingent prompts immediately after awakening to reduce recall bias for P03 and C04–C06.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Pharmacotherapy and invasive neuromodulation (e.g., TMS, implantables) — deprioritized for initial within-person gVAR due to complexity, safety and measurement challenges.
Many overlapping self-regulation constructs (e.g., both progressive muscle relaxation and body-scan) — collapsed into presleep routine or breathing practice to avoid collinearity.
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: A 28-day design with daily morning diaries and one bedtime EMA plus passive nightly aggregates yields sufficient timepoints for person-level gVAR analyses (>100). Keep predictor count to seven to permit stable estimation; monitor missingness and consider Bayesian VAR regularization if necessary.

criteria.label: Nightmare-triggered awakenings (occurrence)
criteria.query_text_used: Nightmare-triggered awakenings (occurrence) | Operational item/signal: Last night, did you wake because of a nightmare? (0=No ; 1=Yes). If yes: How many nightmare awakenings did you have last night? (enter number) AND How much did the nightmare interrupt your sleep overall? (1–9; 1=not at all, 9=completely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Primary event flag and intensity score combined to avoid binary-only measurement; use count when >0.

mapping.rationale: variable label: Nightmare-triggered awakenings (occurrence); measurement: EMA_self_report via morning diary with last-night events (Last night, did you wake because of a nightmare? + count + interference). The leaf nocturnal_awakenings_due_to_nightmares directly encodes awakenings caused by nightmares and is the closest, most specific operational match among candidates for this composite nocturnal nightmare event measure. Other options (REM-specific nightmares, trauma-related nightmares) are not as general or applicable to the variable’s broad scope.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, did you wake because of a nightmare? (0=No ; 1=Yes). If yes: How many nightmare awakenings did you have last night? (enter number) AND How much did the nightmare interrupt your sleep overall? (1–9; 1=not at all, 9=completely)
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: binary flag + integer count + 1–9 interruption severity
measurement.notes: Primary event flag and intensity score combined to avoid binary-only measurement; use count when >0.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: use counts as-is (rare) with a companion severity 1–9 (z-score across series); consider event-flag logistic gVAR or include severity continuous

criteria.label: Nightmare vividness/intensity
criteria.query_text_used: Nightmare vividness/intensity | Operational item/signal: If you had a nightmare last night, how vivid/real did it feel? Rate 1–9 (1=not vivid/like a film, 9=extremely vivid and real) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: If no nightmare flagged, set as missing or 1 with explicit 'no nightmare' flag; prefer severity-only for gVAR continuity.

mapping.rationale: Variable: Nightmare vividness/intensity; Measurement: EMA self-report via morning diary; Item: 'If you had a nightmare last night, how vivid/real did it feel? Rate 1–9 (1=not vivid, 9=extremely vivid)'. The best matching leaf is nightmares_or_vivid_dreams, which explicitly encodes nightmare vividness/vivid dreams and aligns with the measurement focus on intensity of the dream experience (vividness) rather than frequency or distress alone. This provides the most specific operational construct for the intended variable within the available leaves.

measurement.item_or_signal: If you had a nightmare last night, how vivid/real did it feel? Rate 1–9 (1=not vivid/like a film, 9=extremely vivid and real)
measurement.notes: If no nightmare flagged, set as missing or 1 with explicit 'no nightmare' flag; prefer severity-only for gVAR continuity.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score within-person; handle missingness when no nightmare occurred

criteria.label: Anticipatory fear of sleep / reluctance to go to bed
criteria.query_text_used: Anticipatory fear of sleep / reluctance to go to bed | Operational item/signal: Right now (pre-sleep): How fearful or worried are you about going to sleep because of possible nightmares? Rate 1–9 (1=not at all, 9=extremely fearful) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Anchored at bedtime to capture anticipatory fear; also capture planned bedtime and sleep intention as optional contextual items.

mapping.rationale: The variable C03 encodes anticipatory fear/worry about going to sleep due to possible nightmares, assessed momentarily at bedtime via EMA. The closest leaf is bedtime_anxiety_present (sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / sleep-onset insomnia / bedtime_anxiety_present), which represents pre-sleep anxiety at the time of trying to sleep and is aligned with the measure's timing, scale (1–9), and intent. This leaf is more specific than broader nightmare-related or general anxiety nodes and matches the pre-sleep cognitive arousal mechanism targeted by the model.

source_variable.label: Anticipatory fear of sleep / reluctance to go to bed

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now (pre-sleep): How fearful or worried are you about going to sleep because of possible nightmares? Rate 1–9 (1=not at all, 9=extremely fearful)
measurement.notes: Anchored at bedtime to capture anticipatory fear; also capture planned bedtime and sleep intention as optional contextual items.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score within-person

criteria.label: Acute autonomic arousal on awakening
criteria.query_text_used: Acute autonomic arousal on awakening | Operational item/signal: When you woke from the nightmare last night, did you experience sweating or racing heart? Rate intensity 1–9 (1=none, 9=extreme). Additionally: wearable-derived peak heart rate in 5-minute window after awakening (bpm) and HRV short-term estimate (RMSSD when available). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Combine subjective intensity with objective passive spikes aligned to self-reported awakening (±5 min).

mapping.rationale: Variable: Acute autonomic arousal on awakening (subjective sweating or racing heart; wearable HR/HRV post-awakening). The best-matching leaf is autonomic_activation_during_arousal (idx=25859), which explicitly represents autonomic activation during arousal events. This aligns with both the subjective EMA item (sweating/heart racing during awakening) and objective wearable signals (peak HR and RMSSD after awakening). Among candidates, this leaf provides the most specific operational construct for autonomic arousal during awakening from a nightmare, while fitting the measurement modalities described (morning diary EMA + passive HR/HRV data).

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary) + passive wearable HR
measurement.item_or_signal: When you woke from the nightmare last night, did you experience sweating or racing heart? Rate intensity 1–9 (1=none, 9=extreme). Additionally: wearable-derived peak heart rate in 5-minute window after awakening (bpm) and HRV short-term estimate (RMSSD when available).
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: 1–9 Likert + beats per minute + HRV estimate (ms)
measurement.notes: Combine subjective intensity with objective passive spikes aligned to self-reported awakening (±5 min).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: objective HR use log1p for spikes; subjective intensity z-score

criteria.label: Prolonged post-awakening physiological/psychological activation
criteria.query_text_used: Prolonged post-awakening physiological/psychological activation | Operational item/signal: How long did it take you to feel calm/settled after last night's nightmare awakening? Rate 1–9 where 1=0–2 minutes, 5=15–30 minutes, 9=>60 minutes | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Time-to-calm approximated to ordinal bins to reduce skew; can also capture whether participant ultimately returned to sleep last night (yes/no).

mapping.rationale: Variable C05 measures prolonged post-awakening activation (physiological/psychological) reported in a morning diary. The closest, most specific CRITERION leaf is morning_hyperarousal_signs_post_awakening (post-awakening autonomic/cognitive arousal) which aligns with time-to-calm after nightmare awakenings as a proxy for lingering hyperarousal. The measurement item (time to feel calm 1–9) maps to the degree of post-awakening arousal and the morning diary context supports last-night awakening phenomena.

measurement.item_or_signal: How long did it take you to feel calm/settled after last night's nightmare awakening? Rate 1–9 where 1=0–2 minutes, 5=15–30 minutes, 9=>60 minutes
measurement.notes: Time-to-calm approximated to ordinal bins to reduce skew; can also capture whether participant ultimately returned to sleep last night (yes/no).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: treat as ordinal continuous then z-score; consider log-transform if converting to minutes

criteria.label: Difficulty resuming sleep after nightmare awakenings
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty resuming sleep after nightmare awakenings | Operational item/signal: After waking from the nightmare, how difficult was it to fall back asleep? Rate 1–9 (1=fell back asleep immediately, 9=couldn't fall back asleep for long time or not at all). Also: did you eventually return to sleep? (Yes/No) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Use continuous 1–9 as primary gVAR input; binary flag used for event modeling if needed.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 (Difficulty resuming sleep after nightmare awakenings) is a morning-diary EMA item assessing post-awakening sleep maintenance. The closest and most specific CRITERION leaf is wake_after_sleep_onset (idx 26337) under DSM-5TR insomnia disorders / sleep-maintenance insomnia, which directly represents difficulty returning to sleep after nocturnal awakenings. The measurement (1–9 Likert) in a morning diary aligns with capturing wake-after-sleep-onset phenomena at daily resolution in the pseudoprofile context.

source_variable.label: Difficulty resuming sleep after nightmare awakenings

measurement.item_or_signal: After waking from the nightmare, how difficult was it to fall back asleep? Rate 1–9 (1=fell back asleep immediately, 9=couldn't fall back asleep for long time or not at all). Also: did you eventually return to sleep? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Use continuous 1–9 as primary gVAR input; binary flag used for event modeling if needed.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score

predictors.label: Imagery Rehearsal (nightmare imagery rescripting) practice
predictors.query_text_used: Imagery Rehearsal (nightmare imagery rescripting) practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Imagery rehearsal modifies nightmare content and reduces frequency/vividness by providing an alternative memory script and inhibitory learning prior to sleep. | Intervention examples: Perform 10–15 min imagery rehearsal earlier in the evening: rehearse an edited, less threatening version of the nightmare imagery. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, did you do imagery rehearsal (practice changing nightmare content)? (Yes/No). If yes: How fully did you practice it? Rate 1–9 (1=brief/partial, 9=complete session). How many minutes did you practice? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / trauma_memory_imagery_and_narrative_work / imagery_and_rescripting_interventions

mapping.rationale: Direct mapping to the target variable: nightmare_imagery_rehearsal leaf under imagery_and_rescripting_interventions. The variable label explicitly names Imagery Rehearsal (nightmare imagery rescripting) practice, with EMA_self_report on presleep/evening and items about whether practice occurred, adherence intensity, and minutes. This leaf uniquely captures the intended presleep nightmare imagery rehearsal intervention and aligns with the mechanism (modifying nightmare content through rehearsal). It is more specific than broader imagery_rescripting_framework or other imagery-related leaves.

source_variable.label: Imagery Rehearsal (nightmare imagery rescripting) practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Imagery rehearsal modifies nightmare content and reduces frequency/vividness by providing an alternative memory script and inhibitory learning prior to sleep.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Perform 10–15 min imagery rehearsal earlier in the evening: rehearse an edited, less threatening version of the nightmare imagery.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, did you do imagery rehearsal (practice changing nightmare content)? (Yes/No). If yes: How fully did you practice it? Rate 1–9 (1=brief/partial, 9=complete session). How many minutes did you practice?
measurement.notes: Primary behavioral predictor; measure both adherence and intensity.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score adherence intensity; minutes log1p if used

expected_effects.rationale: Imagery rehearsal performed pre-sleep typically reduces next-night nightmare frequency via cognitive updating (effects may appear within 1-2 nights).
Directly targets nightmare content vividness; effect appears on subsequent nights' vividness ratings.
Rehearsal may reduce anticipatory fear at bedtime by increasing perceived control over dreams.
Indirectly may reduce autonomic intensity by altering nightmare content; effect size expected smaller and less immediate.
By reducing nightmare severity, time-to-calm after awakenings may shorten.
Lowered intensity/fear should make resuming sleep easier.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Imagery work can be distressing for some; start with brief practice and provide support resources if distress increases.

feasibility.notes: Self-report adherence is straightforward; effects are episodic but measurable nightly.

predictors.label: Presleep wind-down / sleep routine adherence
predictors.query_text_used: Presleep wind-down / sleep routine adherence | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Consistent presleep routines reduce physiological/cognitive arousal and conditioned anticipatory anxiety, improving sleep maintenance. | Intervention examples: 30–60 min wind-down (low-stimulation activities, no screens, low lighting, gentle reading or audiobook). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Tonight, how well did you follow your wind-down routine? Rate 1–9 (1=not at all, 9=fully). Did you avoid stimulating content and devices in the hour before bed? (Yes/No) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_routines_and_practices / presleep_downshift

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is presleep_downshift / wind_down_routine because the variable label is Presleep wind-down / sleep routine adherence, and the measurement item directly asks how well the wind-down routine was followed (1–9). This leaf represents the most specific operational construct for routine adherence in the presleep_downshift domain. The EMA self-report measurement and the routine-adherence focus align with the measurement mode/item. Mechanistically, presleep wind-down is intended to reduce arousal and anticipatory anxiety, matching the proposed mechanism. The pseudoprofile context treats this as a proximal, modifiable predictor. Other leaves target stimulation avoidance or alternative presleep activities, but wind_down_routine is the closest match to the variable as a whole.

source_variable.label: Presleep wind-down / sleep routine adherence
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Consistent presleep routines reduce physiological/cognitive arousal and conditioned anticipatory anxiety, improving sleep maintenance.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 30–60 min wind-down (low-stimulation activities, no screens, low lighting, gentle reading or audiobook).

measurement.item_or_signal: Tonight, how well did you follow your wind-down routine? Rate 1–9 (1=not at all, 9=fully). Did you avoid stimulating content and devices in the hour before bed? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Routine adherence is a proximal modifiable predictor; use both adherence intensity and binary avoidance items.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; binary items as proportion over windows

expected_effects.rationale: Better wind-down reduces general arousal and may reduce night awakenings.
May modestly reduce dream vividness by lowering presleep cognitive activation.
Directly reduces anticipatory fear via calming routines.
Lower baseline arousal reduces peak autonomic response probability/intensity.
Calmer baseline may shorten time-to-calm after awakenings.
Improved sleep drive and conditioning makes returning to sleep easier.

feasibility.notes: Simple nightly self-report; high adherence expected if kept brief.

predictors.label: Post-awakening grounding and paced-breathing practice
predictors.query_text_used: Post-awakening grounding and paced-breathing practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Grounding and paced breathing applied immediately after awakening target autonomic downregulation, reducing heart-rate spikes and shortening time-to-calm. | Intervention examples: 3–10 minutes of 6-breath-per-minute paced breathing and grounding (5-4-3-2-1 sensory check), performed on awakening. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | After the awakening, did you use grounding or paced breathing? (Yes/No). If yes: Rate how long/intense (1–9; 1=brief, 9=full practice). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / breath_and_relaxation_skills

mapping.rationale: The variable is 'Post-awakening grounding and paced-breathing practice' with EMA self-report measurement after awakening (Yes/No and intensity). Among candidates, the closest exact match is paced_breathing_practice (a specific breath-and-relaxation skill) under breath_and_relaxation_skills within PSYCHO, aligning with the measurement item (Yes/No + intensity) and the morning diary context. While grounding components exist (grounding_practice/grounding), there is no leaf that explicitly combines grounding + paced breathing; pacer breathing is the most specific operational construct for the breathing component described. This mapping uses the measurement mode (EMA_self_report) and the last-awakening recall window appropriately.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / paced_breathing_practice

source_variable.label: Post-awakening grounding and paced-breathing practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Grounding and paced breathing applied immediately after awakening target autonomic downregulation, reducing heart-rate spikes and shortening time-to-calm.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 3–10 minutes of 6-breath-per-minute paced breathing and grounding (5-4-3-2-1 sensory check), performed on awakening.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary) + optional event-trigger
measurement.item_or_signal: After the awakening, did you use grounding or paced breathing? (Yes/No). If yes: Rate how long/intense (1–9; 1=brief, 9=full practice).
measurement.notes: Event-tied practice expected to affect immediate arousal metrics; capture timing relative to awakening when possible.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score intensity; use binary flag for event analysis

expected_effects.rationale: Unlikely to reduce occurrence of nightmares (which originate during REM) but may influence downstream outcomes; therefore effect on C01 is uncertain.
Direct influence on nightmare vividness less likely; immediate effect is arousal reduction.
Practicing grounding may reduce anticipatory fear by increasing perceived coping ability in later nights.
Direct autonomic downregulation reduces heart rate/sweating intensity within minutes.
Shortens time-to-calm by lowering physiological arousal quickly.
Facilitates returning to sleep by reducing activation and intrusive imagery.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: If practice increases distress for some, reduce duration and provide alternative grounding prompts.

feasibility.notes: Event-linked measure; may miss some awakenings if participant delayed diary entry—encourage quick post-awakening reporting.

predictors.label: Sleep environment optimization (light/noise/temperature/comfort)
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep environment optimization (light/noise/temperature/comfort) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Reducing environmental disruptions and improving comfort reduces likelihood of awakenings and facilitates returning to sleep; may also reduce conditioned arousal. | Intervention examples: Use blackout curtains, lower bedroom temperature, reduce ambient noise, remove screens from bedroom, use low-path night lights for bathroom. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Rate last night's bedroom comfort/optimization (temperature, light, noise, mattress) overall 1–9 (1=very poor, 9=excellent). Optional: was a low-light bathroom path used? (Yes/No) | Prior ontology hint: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_environment

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf is BIO / environmental_exposures_and_built_environment_optimization / thermal_comfort_and_shelter_integrity / indoor_sleep_comfort_environment / bedding_thermal_optimization. The var label Sleep environment optimization and the EMA self-report measure (rating last night's bedroom comfort/optimization including temperature, light, noise, mattress) map directly to environmental comfort and bedding thermals. The mechanism is that reducing environmental disruptions lowers awakenings and facilitates returning to sleep. This is the most comprehensive environmental predictor among candidates (others target specific aspects like blue light, blackout, or mattress alone) and fits the morning diary assessment window. Pseudoprofile context provides general neurological/physiological arousal and sleep initiation dynamics but is not overfitting the measurement choice.

source_variable.label: Sleep environment optimization (light/noise/temperature/comfort)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Reducing environmental disruptions and improving comfort reduces likelihood of awakenings and facilitates returning to sleep; may also reduce conditioned arousal.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Use blackout curtains, lower bedroom temperature, reduce ambient noise, remove screens from bedroom, use low-path night lights for bathroom.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary) + optional passive phone ambient light/noise if available
measurement.item_or_signal: Rate last night's bedroom comfort/optimization (temperature, light, noise, mattress) overall 1–9 (1=very poor, 9=excellent). Optional: was a low-light bathroom path used? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Passive ambient noise/light measures can augment self-report when available.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: z-score; treat as slowly-varying covariate if largely constant

expected_effects.rationale: Fewer environmental disruptors make awakenings less likely and reduce secondary awakenings.
Effect on dream vividness is indirect and likely small (hence nonlinear/uncertain).
A comfortable environment reduces pre-sleep vigilance and anticipatory fear.
Lower external triggers can reduce reflexive autonomic responses on awakening.
Better sleep environment supports faster return to baseline after awakening.
Comfort and reduced light/noise support falling back asleep sooner.

feasibility.notes: If environment is near-constant, may show low variance—encourage small nightly changes to increase signal.

predictors.label: HRV / slow-breathing biofeedback practice and passive HRV monitoring
predictors.query_text_used: HRV / slow-breathing biofeedback practice and passive HRV monitoring | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Regular HRV/slow-breathing practice increases parasympathetic tone and reduces autonomic reactivity; passive HR/HRV tracks physiological arousal during/after awakenings. | Intervention examples: Daily 10-minute paced-breathing biofeedback or guided HRV exercises; wearable records nightly HR and short-term HRV (RMSSD). | Measurement: wearable_sensor | Passive: nightly mean HR, peak HR in awakening windows, and RMSSD per-night. EMA: Did you practice HRV biofeedback today? (Yes/No) If yes, duration minutes and perceived effectiveness 1–9. | Prior ontology hint: BIO / neuromodulation_and_neurostimulation / biofeedback_and_neurofeedback

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: HRV biofeedback device. The variable combines HRV metrics collected by wearables (nightly HR, RMSSD) with a paced-breathing biofeedback component (biofeedback practice). The leaf 'hrv_biofeedback_device' explicitly captures a wearable biofeedback device for HRV-based autonomic regulation, aligning with both passive HRV monitoring and active biofeedback training described in the variable. Other candidates (hrv_tracker, hrv_guided_breathing_tool, chest-strap_hrv, etc.) are more narrow (tracking only, or guided breathing only) and do not fully encompass both the passive HRV monitoring and active HRV biofeedback component described.
mapping.leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / autonomic_and_cardiovascular / heart_rate_and_hrv / hrv_biofeedback_device

source_variable.label: HRV / slow-breathing biofeedback practice and passive HRV monitoring
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Regular HRV/slow-breathing practice increases parasympathetic tone and reduces autonomic reactivity; passive HR/HRV tracks physiological arousal during/after awakenings.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Daily 10-minute paced-breathing biofeedback or guided HRV exercises; wearable records nightly HR and short-term HRV (RMSSD).

measurement.channel: wearable device (heart rate, accelerometry) + smartphone app for practice logging
measurement.item_or_signal: Passive: nightly mean HR, peak HR in awakening windows, and RMSSD per-night. EMA: Did you practice HRV biofeedback today? (Yes/No) If yes, duration minutes and perceived effectiveness 1–9.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: HR bpm, HRV ms; binary + minutes + 1–9
measurement.recall_window: daily aggregate (last 24 hours) and per-awakening windows
measurement.notes: Objective physiological measures align with subjective intensity items for C04 and C05.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: RMSSD use log or winsorize; BPM subject to z-score and remove measurement artifacts

expected_effects.rationale: Training may reduce baseline reactivity over days, possibly lowering awakening probability indirectly; effect timeline can be multi-day.
Little direct effect on dream content, likely small/indirect.
Improved physiological control can reduce anticipatory hyperarousal at bedtime.
Immediate practice before bed or on awakening can lower autonomic spikes; passive HRV correlates with arousal intensity.
Improved parasympathetic tone shortens time-to-calm.
Lower arousal supports faster resumption of sleep.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure wearable data privacy; advise pause if biofeedback practice increases anxiety.

feasibility.notes: Passive data dependent on participant having compatible wearable; otherwise use self-report practice logs.

predictors.label: Stimulus-control / non-clock-checking and bed association
predictors.query_text_used: Stimulus-control / non-clock-checking and bed association | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Reducing bed-related worry/clock checking and re-establishing bed as sleep-only environment reduces conditioned arousal and anticipatory anxiety. | Intervention examples: Avoid checking clock/phone when awake at night; get out of bed if unable to return to sleep and perform a quiet activity. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Last night, did you check the clock or your phone during awakenings? (Yes/No). How often? Rate 1–9 frequency (1=never, 9=very often). Did you get out of bed if unable to sleep? (Yes/No) | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / conditioning_and_associations / stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf: stimulus_control_bed_association_relearning. The variable is labeled as Stimulus-control / non-clock-checking and bed association, which directly corresponds to a CBT-I oriented stimulus-control intervention. The measurement items (Last night, did you check the clock or your phone during awakenings? How often? 1–9; Did you get out of bed if unable to sleep?) align with EMA/self-report morning diary capture of clock-checking and bed-exit behaviors. Mechanism: re-establishing bed as sleep-only to reduce conditioned arousal and anticipatory worry. Pseudoprofile context notes this predictor among seven action-oriented factors; the measurement mode (EMA via smartphone, morning diary) and unit (binary plus 1–9 frequency) match the operational construct closely.

source_variable.label: Stimulus-control / non-clock-checking and bed association
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Reducing bed-related worry/clock checking and re-establishing bed as sleep-only environment reduces conditioned arousal and anticipatory anxiety.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Avoid checking clock/phone when awake at night; get out of bed if unable to return to sleep and perform a quiet activity.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (morning diary / event if awakened)
measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, did you check the clock or your phone during awakenings? (Yes/No). How often? Rate 1–9 frequency (1=never, 9=very often). Did you get out of bed if unable to sleep? (Yes/No)
measurement.notes: Stimulus control behaviors directly map to conditioned arousal and difficulty returning to sleep.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: binary behaviors as proportions; frequency as z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Reducing active engagement/clock-checking may reduce secondary awakenings and conditioned awakening responses.
Effect on vividness is indirect and small.
Reduces bedtime hypervigilance and threat-checking behaviors.
Reducing checking can attenuate sympathetic activation triggered by threat cues (clock/time anxiety).
Less checking and better stimulus control supports faster return to sleep.
Direct behavioral pathway to improve resumption of sleep.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Encourage safe behaviors (avoid wandering alone if distressed).

feasibility.notes: Self-report items easy to collect nightly; passive phone unlock logs could augment if consent provided.

predictors.label: Wearable sleep/arousal monitoring with feedback
predictors.query_text_used: Wearable sleep/arousal monitoring with feedback | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Objective detection of awakenings and autonomic spikes supports measurement-based feedback and may improve adherence to coping strategies when used for self-monitoring. | Intervention examples: Wear a wrist sensor to capture sleep/wake, accelerometry, and heart rate; receive nightly summary with detected awakenings and peak HR metrics. | Measurement: wearable_sensor | Per-night: number of device-detected awakenings, total awake minutes, peak HR in awakening windows (bpm), nightly mean HR, and estimated sleep stages if available. | Prior ontology hint: BIO / personalization_and_monitoring / wearables_and_home_monitoring

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is the wrist-worn sleep tracker in BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / sleep_and_circadian / wearables / sleep_tracker_wrist_wearable. The variable Wearable sleep/arousal monitoring with feedback maps to a wearable sleep-tracking device that detects awakenings and HR-related arousal at nightly resolution and can deliver feedback to the user, aligning with per-night measurements ( awakenings, minutes awake, peak HR, nightly mean HR, sleep stages ) and the measurement mode (wearable_sensor) with feedback context in the pseudoprofile.
mapping.leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / sleep_and_circadian / wearables / sleep_tracker_wrist_wearable

source_variable.label: Wearable sleep/arousal monitoring with feedback
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Objective detection of awakenings and autonomic spikes supports measurement-based feedback and may improve adherence to coping strategies when used for self-monitoring.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Wear a wrist sensor to capture sleep/wake, accelerometry, and heart rate; receive nightly summary with detected awakenings and peak HR metrics.

measurement.item_or_signal: Per-night: number of device-detected awakenings, total awake minutes, peak HR in awakening windows (bpm), nightly mean HR, and estimated sleep stages if available.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: counts (awakenings), minutes awake, bpm, HRV when available
measurement.recall_window: nightly aggregate aligned to morning diary
measurement.notes: Monitoring itself may change behavior (reactivity); include self-report of whether feedback was reviewed each day.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: handle device-specific offsets; z-score nightly metrics, winsorize extreme spikes

expected_effects.rationale: Feedback may indirectly reduce awakenings by informing behavior changes; direct measurement maps well to this criterion.
Wearable data does not directly measure dream vividness; possible indirect effects via behavior change.
Seeing objective data may reduce catastrophizing and anticipatory fear via psychoeducation/feedback.
Objective detection of HR spikes maps to autonomic arousal and can inform immediate downregulation strategies.
Feedback can prompt use of quicker regulation strategies, reducing time-to-calm.
Monitoring+feedback supports behavior change (stimulus control, routines) and may improve resumption of sleep.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure privacy and informed consent for continuous biometric data; data may cause rumination in some users—monitor for increased worry.

feasibility.notes: Device availability and data quality vary; include fallbacks to self-report if passive data missing.
